SpaceX Falcon 9 First Stage
Landing Prediction

Benson Cyril Nana Boakye
IBM Applied Data Science Capstone Project
90
Total Launches
66.7%
Success Rate
94.4%
Best Accuracy
Decision Tree
Best Algorithm
Overview 1A: API 1B: Scraping 2: Wrangling 3A: EDA Viz 3B: SQL 4: Folium 5: ML Findings Tech Stack
Section 1

Project Overview

Business Context

SpaceX has disrupted the commercial space industry by dramatically reducing launch costs through rocket reusability. The Falcon 9 first stage booster — worth approximately $62 million per mission — can be recovered, refurbished, and re-flown, enabling SpaceX to offer launches at roughly $67 million compared to competitors charging over $165 million.

Predicting whether the first stage will successfully land is critical for cost estimation, mission planning, and competitive intelligence. This project builds machine learning models to predict Falcon 9 first stage landing outcomes using publicly available data.

Project Objectives

  • Collect Falcon 9 launch data from the SpaceX REST API and Wikipedia via web scraping
  • Clean, wrangle, and engineer features from raw launch records
  • Perform exploratory data analysis using matplotlib, seaborn, SQL, Folium, and Plotly Dash
  • Build and tune classification models to predict first stage landing success
  • Identify the best-performing algorithm and key predictive features

Dataset Description

The dataset contains 90 Falcon 9 launches with the following key features:

  • FlightNumber — sequential launch identifier
  • BoosterVersion — version of the Falcon 9 booster (v1.0, v1.1, FT, B4, B5)
  • PayloadMass — payload mass in kilograms
  • Orbit — target orbit type (LEO, GTO, ISS, SSO, etc.)
  • LaunchSite — CCAFS SLC 40, KSC LC 39A, VAFB SLC 4E
  • Outcome — booster landing result
  • class — binary target: 1 = successful landing, 0 = failed

Methodology: 5 Phases

  1. Phase 1A: Data Collection via SpaceX REST API
  2. Phase 1B: Data Collection via Web Scraping (Wikipedia)
  3. Phase 2: Data Wrangling and Feature Engineering
  4. Phase 3: Exploratory Data Analysis (Visualization + SQL)
  5. Phase 4: Interactive Visual Analytics (Folium + Plotly Dash)
  6. Phase 5: Predictive Analysis — Classification Models
Phase 1A

Data Collection: SpaceX REST API

Approach

Used the SpaceX v4 REST API to fetch historical launch records, then enriched each record by making additional API calls for rocket details, payload mass, launchpad coordinates, and core (booster) landing outcomes. A static JSON snapshot was used for reproducibility.

Cell 16

# Takes the dataset and uses the rocket column to call the API and append the data to the list
def getBoosterVersion(data):
    for x in data['rocket']:
        response = requests.get("https://api.spacexdata.com/v4/rockets/"+str(x)).json()
        BoosterVersion.append(response['name'])

Cell 18

# Takes the dataset and uses the launchpad column to call the API and append the data to the list
def getLaunchSite(data):
    for x in data['launchpad']:
        response = requests.get("https://api.spacexdata.com/v4/launchpads/"+str(x)).json()
        Longitude.append(response['longitude'])
        Latitude.append(response['latitude'])
        LaunchSite.append(response['name'])

Cell 20

# Takes the dataset and uses the payloads column to call the API and append the data to the lists
def getPayloadData(data):
    for load in data['payloads']:
        response = requests.get("https://api.spacexdata.com/v4/payloads/"+load).json()
        PayloadMass.append(response['mass_kg'])
        Orbit.append(response['orbit'])

Cell 22

# Takes the dataset and uses the cores column to call the API and append the data to the lists
def getCoreData(data):
    for core in data['cores']:
            if core['core'] != None:
                response = requests.get("https://api.spacexdata.com/v4/cores/"+core['core']).json()
                Block.append(response['block'])
                ReusedCount.append(response['reuse_count'])
                Serial.append(response['serial'])
            else:
                Block.append(None)
                ReusedCount.append(None)
                Serial.append(None)
            Outcome.append(str(core['landing_success'])+' '+str(core['landing_type']))
            Flights.append(core['flight'])
            GridFins.append(core['gridfins'])
            Reused.append(core['reused'])
            Legs.append(core['legs'])
            LandingPad.append(core['landpad'])

Cell 24

spacex_url="https://api.spacexdata.com/v4/launches/past"

Cell 36

# Use json_normalize method to convert the json result into a dataframe
data = pd.json_normalize(response.json())

Cell 40

# Lets take a subset of our dataframe keeping only the features we want and the flight number, and date_utc.
data = data[['rocket', 'payloads', 'launchpad', 'cores', 'flight_number', 'date_utc']]

# We will remove rows with multiple cores because those are falcon rockets with 2 extra rocket boosters and rows that have multiple payloads in a single rocket.
data = data[data['cores'].map(len)==1]
data = data[data['payloads'].map(len)==1]

# Since payloads and cores are lists of size 1 we will also extract the single value in the list and replace the feature.
data['cores'] = data['cores'].map(lambda x : x[0])
data['payloads'] = data['payloads'].map(lambda x : x[0])

# We also want to convert the date_utc to a datetime datatype and then extracting the date leaving the time
data['date'] = pd.to_datetime(data['date_utc']).dt.date

# Using the date we will restrict the dates of the launches
data = data[data['date'] <= datetime.date(2020, 11, 13)]

Cell 42

#Global variables 
BoosterVersion = []
PayloadMass = []
Orbit = []
LaunchSite = []
Outcome = []
Flights = []
GridFins = []
Reused = []
Legs = []
LandingPad = []
Block = []
ReusedCount = []
Serial = []
Longitude = []
Latitude = []

92 89 2020-10-24 Falcon 9 15600.0 VLEO CCSFS SLC 40 True ASDS 3 True True True 5e9e3033383ecbb9e534e7cc 5.0 8 B1060 -80.577366 28.561857
93 90 2020-11-05 Falcon 9 3681.0 MEO CCSFS SLC 40 True ASDS 1 True False True 5e9e3032383ecb6bb234e7ca 5.0 2 B1062 -80.577366 28.561857

90 rows × 17 columns

Phase 1B

Data Collection: Web Scraping

Approach

Used BeautifulSoup to scrape the Wikipedia page listing all Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy launches. The HTML table was parsed to extract flight numbers, dates, booster versions, launch sites, payloads, orbits, customers, launch outcomes, and booster landing results.

Cell 15

import sys

import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import re
import unicodedata
import pandas as pd

Cell 17

def date_time(table_cells):
    """
    This function returns the data and time from the HTML  table cell
    Input: the  element of a table data cell extracts extra row
    """
    return [data_time.strip() for data_time in list(table_cells.strings)][0:2]

def booster_version(table_cells):
    """
    This function returns the booster version from the HTML  table cell 
    Input: the  element of a table data cell extracts extra row
    """
    out=''.join([booster_version for i,booster_version in enumerate( table_cells.strings) if i%2==0][0:-1])
    return out

def landing_status(table_cells):
    """
    This function returns the landing status from the HTML table cell 
    Input: the  element of a table data cell extracts extra row
    """
    out=[i for i in table_cells.strings][0]
    return out


def get_mass(table_cells):
    mass=unicodedata.normalize("NFKD", table_cells.text).strip()
    if mass:
        mass.find("kg")
        new_mass=mass[0:mass.find("kg")+2]
    else:
        new_mass=0
    return new_mass


def extract_column_from_header(row):
    """
    This function returns the landing status from the HTML table cell 
    Input: the  element of a table data cell extracts extra row
    """
    if (row.br):
        row.br.extract()
    if row.a:
        row.a.extract()
    if row.sup:
        row.sup.extract()
        
    colunm_name = ' '.join(row.contents)
    
    # Filter the digit and empty names
    if not(colunm_name.strip().isdigit()):
        colunm_name = colunm_name.strip()
        return colunm_name

Cell 19

static_url = "https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=List_of_Falcon_9_and_Falcon_Heavy_launches&oldid=1027686922"

Cell 23

# use requests.get() method with the provided static_url
response = requests.get(static_url)
# assign the response to a object
data = response.text

Cell 25

# Use BeautifulSoup() to create a BeautifulSoup object from a response text content
soup = BeautifulSoup(data, 'html5lib')

Cell 27

# Use soup.title attribute
print(soup.title)
Stream Output
<title>List of Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy launches - Wikipedia</title>

Cell 31

# Use the find_all function in the BeautifulSoup object, with element type `table`
# Assign the result to a list called `html_tables`
html_tables = soup.find_all('table')

Cell 33

# Let's print the third table and check its content
first_launch_table = html_tables[2]
print(first_launch_table)
Stream Output
<table class="wikitable plainrowheaders collapsible" style="width: 100%;">
<tbody><tr>
<th scope="col">Flight No.
</th>
<th scope="col">Date and<br/>time (<a href="/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time" title="Coordinated Universal Time">UTC</a>)
</th>
<th scope="col"><a href="/wiki/List_of_Falcon_9_first-stage_boosters" title="List of Falcon 9 first-stage boosters">Version,<br/>Booster</a> <sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-booster_11-0"><a href="#cite_note-booster-11">[b]</a></sup>
</th>
<th scope="col">Launch site
</th>
<th scope="col">Payload<sup class="reference" id="cite_ref-Dragon_12-0"><a 
... [truncated]

Cell 37

column_names = []

# Apply find_all() function with `th` element on first_launch_table
# Iterate each th element and apply the provided extract_column_from_header() to get a column name
# Append the Non-empty column name (`if name is not None and len(name) > 0`) into a list called column_names

for row in first_launch_table.find_all('th'):
    name = extract_column_from_header(row)
    if(name != None and len(name) > 0):
        column_names.append(name)

Cell 39

print(column_names)
Stream Output
['Flight No.', 'Date and time ( )', 'Launch site', 'Payload', 'Payload mass', 'Orbit', 'Customer', 'Launch outcome']

Cell 46

extracted_row = 0
#Extract each table 
for table_number,table in enumerate(soup.find_all('table',"wikitable plainrowheaders collapsible")):
   # get table row 
    for rows in table.find_all("tr"):
        #check to see if first table heading is as number corresponding to launch a number 
        if rows.th:
            if rows.th.string:
                flight_number=rows.th.string.strip()
                flag=flight_number.isdigit()
        else:
            flag=False
        #get table element 
        row=rows.find_all('td')
        #if it is number save cells in a dictonary 
        if flag:
            extracted_row += 1
            # Flight Number value
            # Append the flight_number into launch_dict with key `Flight No.`
            launch_dict["Flight No."].append(flight_number)
                                   
            # Date value
            #Append the date into launch_dict with key `Date`
            datatimelist=date_time(row[0])
            date = datatimelist[0].strip(',')
            launch_dict["Date"].append(date)
            
            # Time value
            #Append the time into launch_dict with key `Time`
            time = datatimelist[1]
            launch_dict["Time"].append(time)
              
            # Booster version
            #Append the bv into launch_dict with key `Version Booster`
            bv=booster_version(row[1])
            if not(bv):
                bv=row[1].a.string
            launch_dict["Version Booster"].append(bv)
            
            # Launch Site
            #Append the bv into launch_dict with key `Launch site`
            launch_site = row[2].a.string
            launch_dict['Launch site'].append(launch_site)
            
            # Payload
            #Append the payload into launch_dict with key `Payload`
            payload = row[3].a.string
            launch_dict['Payload'].append(payload)
            
            # Payload Mass
            #Append the payload_mass into launch_dict with key `Payload mass`
            payload_mass = get_mass(row[4])
            launch_dict['Payload mass'].append(payload_mass)
            
            # Orbit
            #Append the orbit into launch_dict with key `Orbit`
            orbit = row[5].a.string
            launch_dict['Orbit'].append(orbit)
            
            # Customer
            #Append the customer into launch_dict with key `Customer`
            if row[6].a != None:
                customer = row[6].a.string
            else:
                customer = 'None'
                
            launch_dict['Customer'].append(customer)
                        
            # Launch outcome
            #Append the launch_outcome into launch_dict with key `Launch outcome`
            launch_outcome = list(row[7].strings)[0]
            launch_dict['Launch outcome'].append(launch_outcome)
                        
            # Booster landing
            #Append the booster landing into launch_dict with key `Booster landing`
            booster_landing = landing_status(row[8])
            launch_dict['Booster landing'].append(booster_landing)
            
            
            print(f"Flight Number: {flight_number}, Date: {date}, Time: {time} \n \
            Booster Version {bv}, Launch Site: {launch_site} \n \
            Payload: {payload}, Orbit: {orbit} \n \
            Customer: {customer}, Launch Outcome: {launch_outcome}\
            Booster Landing: {booster_landing} \n \
            *** ")
Stream Output
Flight Number: 1, Date: 4 June 2010, Time: 18:45 
             Booster Version F9 v1.0B0003.1, Launch Site: CCAFS 
             Payload: Dragon Spacecraft Qualification Unit, Orbit: LEO 
             Customer: SpaceX, Launch Outcome: Success
            Booster Landing: Failure 
             *** 
Flight Number: 2, Date: 8 December 2010, Time: 15:43 
             Booster Version F9 v1.0B0004.1, Launch Site: CCAFS 
             Payload: Dragon, Orbit: LEO 
             Customer: NASA, Launch Outcome: Success            Booster Landing: Failure 
             *** 
Flight Number: 3, Date: 22 May 20
... [truncated]
Phase 2

Data Wrangling

Approach

Cleaned the raw dataset by handling missing values, filtering to Falcon 9 launches only, calculating landing class labels, encoding categorical variables, and standardizing features for ML modeling.

Cell 14

# Pandas is a software library written for the Python programming language for data manipulation and analysis.
import pandas as pd
#NumPy is a library for the Python programming language, adding support for large, multi-dimensional arrays and matrices, along with a large collection of high-level mathematical functions to operate on these arrays
import numpy as np

Cell 40

# landing_class = 0 if bad_outcome
# landing_class = 1 otherwise

landing_class = []

for outcome in df['Outcome']:
    if outcome in bad_outcomes:
        landing_class.append(0)
    else:
        landing_class.append(1)

Phase 3A

Exploratory Data Analysis: Visualization

Approach

Used matplotlib and seaborn to visualize relationships between launch features and landing outcomes. Charts explore how flight number, payload mass, orbit type, launch site, and year correlate with success rates.

Cell 14

#Pandas is a software library written for the Python programming language for data manipulation and analysis.
import pandas as pd
#NumPy is a library for the Python programming language, adding support for large, multi-dimensional arrays and matrices, along with a large collection of high-level mathematical functions to operate on these arrays
import numpy as np
# Matplotlib is a plotting library for python and pyplot gives us a MatLab like plotting framework. We will use this in our plotter function to plot data.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
#Seaborn is a Python data visualization library based on matplotlib. It provides a high-level interface for drawing attractive and informative statistical graphics
import seaborn as sns

Cell 16

sns.set_style("dark")

Cell 19

df=pd.read_csv("https://cf-courses-data.s3.us.cloud-object-storage.appdomain.cloud/IBM-DS0321EN-SkillsNetwork/datasets/dataset_part_2.csv")

Cell 21

sns.catplot(y="PayloadMass", x="FlightNumber", hue="Class", data=df, aspect = 5)

plt.ylabel("Pay load Mass (kg)",fontsize=20)
plt.show()
Flight Number vs. Launch Site — Distribution of launches across CCAFS SLC 40, KSC LC 39A, and VAFB SLC 4E over time

Cell 26

sns.set_palette("bright")

Cell 27

# Plot a scatter point chart with x axis to be Flight Number and y axis to be the launch site, and hue to be the class value
sns.catplot(data=df, x="FlightNumber", y="LaunchSite", hue="Class")
plt.ylabel("Launch Site",fontsize=15)
plt.xlabel("Flight Number",fontsize=15)
plt.show()
Payload Mass vs. Launch Site — Payload mass ranges for each launch site with landing outcome color-coding

Cell 31

# Plot a scatter point chart with x axis to be Pay Load Mass (kg) and y axis to be the launch site, and hue to be the class value
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x="PayloadMass", y="LaunchSite", hue="Class")
plt.show()
Success Rate vs. Orbit Type — Comparison of landing success rates across different orbit types (GTO, SSO, ISS, LEO, etc.)

Cell 36

# HINT use groupby method on Orbit column and get the mean of Class column
df_orbitsuccess = df.groupby('Orbit').mean().reset_index()

sns.barplot(data=df_orbitsuccess, x='Orbit', y='Class')
plt.show()
Flight Number vs. Orbit Type — How orbit type distribution changed across flight numbers

Cell 40

# Plot a scatter point chart with x axis to be FlightNumber and y axis to be the Orbit, and hue to be the class value
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='FlightNumber', y='Orbit', hue='Class')
plt.show()
Payload Mass vs. Orbit Type — Relationship between payload mass and orbit type with success indicators

Cell 44

# Plot a scatter point chart with x axis to be Payload and y axis to be the Orbit, and hue to be the class value
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='PayloadMass', y='Orbit', hue='Class')
plt.show()
Launch Success Rate by Year — Trend in landing success rates from 2010 to 2020

Cell 49

# A function to Extract years from the date 
year=[]
def Extract_year(date):
    for i in df["Date"]:
        year.append(i.split("-")[0])
    return year

Cell 50

df['Year'] = Extract_year('Date')

Cell 51

# Plot a line chart with x axis to be the extracted year and y axis to be the success rate
sns.lineplot(data=df, x='Year', y='Class')
plt.show()
Feature Correlation Heatmap — Correlation matrix of engineered dummy variables used as ML features

Cell 63

# HINT: use astype function
features_one_hot = features_one_hot.astype('float64')

Cell 65

features_one_hot.to_csv('dataset_part\_3.csv', index=False)
Phase 3B

Exploratory Data Analysis: SQL

Approach

Loaded the SpaceX dataset into an IBM Db2 cloud database and executed 10 SQL queries using the ipython-sql extension and SQLAlchemy. Queries explored unique launch sites, payload totals, mission outcomes, booster versions, and landing results by date range.

Query 1: All unique launch site names

SQL Query
SELECT UNIQUE(LAUNCH_SITE) FROM SPACEXTBL;
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.
Query Result
launch_site
CCAFS LC-40
CCAFS SLC-40
KSC LC-39A
VAFB SLC-4E

Query 2: Launch sites starting with 'CCA'

SQL Query
SELECT LAUNCH_SITE FROM SPACEXTBL WHERE LAUNCH_SITE LIKE 'CCA%' LIMIT 5;
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.
Query Result
launch_site
CCAFS LC-40
CCAFS LC-40
CCAFS LC-40
CCAFS LC-40
CCAFS LC-40

Query 3: Total payload mass for NASA CRS missions

SQL Query
SELECT SUM(PAYLOAD_MASS__KG_) AS TOTAL_PAYLOAD_MASS FROM SPACEXTBL WHERE CUSTOMER = 'NASA (CRS)';
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.
Query Result
total_payload_mass
45596

Query 4: Average payload mass for F9 v1.1 booster

SQL Query
SELECT AVG(PAYLOAD_MASS__KG_) AS AVERAGE_PAYLOAD_MASS FROM SPACEXTBL WHERE BOOSTER_VERSION = 'F9 v1.1';
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.
Query Result
average_payload_mass
2928

Query 5: Date of first successful ground pad landing

SQL Query
SELECT MIN(DATE) AS FIRST_SUCCESSFUL_GROUND_LANDING FROM SPACEXTBL WHERE LANDING__OUTCOME = 'Success (ground pad)';
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.
Query Result
first_successful_ground_landing
2015-12-22

Query 6: Boosters that landed on drone ship with payload 4000-6000 kg

SQL Query
SELECT BOOSTER_VERSION FROM SPACEXTBL WHERE (LANDING__OUTCOME = 'Success (drone ship)') AND (PAYLOAD_MASS__KG_ BETWEEN 4000 AND 6000);
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.
Query Result
booster_version
F9 FT B1022
F9 FT B1026
F9 FT B1021.2
F9 FT B1031.2

Query 7: Count of each mission outcome type

SQL Query
SELECT MISSION_OUTCOME, COUNT(MISSION_OUTCOME) AS TOTAL_NUMBER FROM SPACEXTBL GROUP BY MISSION_OUTCOME;
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.
Query Result
mission_outcome total_number
Failure (in flight) 1
Success 99
Success (payload status unclear) 1

Query 8: Booster versions that carried the maximum payload

SQL Query
SELECT DISTINCT(BOOSTER_VERSION) FROM SPACEXTBL WHERE PAYLOAD_MASS__KG_ = (SELECT MAX(PAYLOAD_MASS__KG_) FROM SPACEXTBL);
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.

F9 B5 B1051.4
F9 B5 B1051.6

F9 B5 B1060.3

Query 9: 2015 drone ship landing failures

SQL Query
SELECT BOOSTER_VERSION, LAUNCH_SITE FROM SPACEXTBL WHERE (LANDING__OUTCOME = 'Failure (drone ship)') AND (EXTRACT(YEAR FROM DATE) = '2015');
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb

booster_version launch_site

Query 10: Landing outcome counts from 2010 to March 2017

SQL Query
SELECT LANDING__OUTCOME, COUNT(LANDING__OUTCOME) AS TOTAL_NUMBER FROM SPACEXTBL WHERE DATE BETWEEN '2010-06-04' AND '2017-03-20' GROUP BY LANDING__OUTCOME ORDER BY TOTAL_NUMBER DESC;
Output
 * ibm_db_sa://kfm42587:***@3883e7e4-18f5-4afe-be8c-fa31c41761d2.bs2io90l08kqb1od8lcg.databases.appdomain.cloud:31498/bludb
Done.

landing__outcome total_number
No attempt 10
Failure (drone ship) 5
Success (drone ship) 5
Controlled (ocean) 3
Success (ground pad) 3
Failure (parachute) 2
Uncontrolled (ocean) 2
Precluded (drone ship) 1
Phase 4

Interactive Visual Analytics: Folium and Plotly Dash

Folium Interactive Maps

Used Folium to create interactive maps showing the geographic distribution of SpaceX launch sites. Launch outcomes were color-coded (green = success, red = failure) with marker clusters for density visualization. Distance calculations from each site to the nearest coastline, highway, and railway were performed.

SpaceX Launch Sites — Static map showing all four launch sites: CCAFS SLC 40 (Florida), KSC LC 39A (Florida), VAFB SLC 4E (California). All sites are situated near coastlines for safety and trajectory optimization.
Folium Interactive Map Screenshot — Color-coded marker clusters showing launch success (green) and failure (red) rates at each site. KSC LC 39A shows the highest success concentration.

Cell 18

# Select relevant sub-columns: `Launch Site`, `Lat(Latitude)`, `Long(Longitude)`, `class`
spacex_df = spacex_df[['Launch Site', 'Lat', 'Long', 'class']]
launch_sites_df = spacex_df.groupby(['Launch Site'], as_index=False).first()
launch_sites_df = launch_sites_df[['Launch Site', 'Lat', 'Long']]
launch_sites_df

# Start location is NASA Johnson Space Center
nasa_coordinate = [29.559684888503615, -95.0830971930759]

#Initialise the map
site_map = folium.Map(location=nasa_coordinate, zoom_start=12)

#define the circle (blue circle at NASA JSC)
nasa_circle = folium.Circle(nasa_coordinate, radius=1000, color='#001cd3', fill=True).add_child(folium.Popup('NASA Johnson Space Center')) #popup label of name

#Add the NASA circle to the map
site_map.add_child(nasa_circle)

#show the map
site_map
DataFrame (Cell 22)
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Cell 28

# Initialise the map
site_map = folium.Map(location=nasa_coordinate, zoom_start=5)

# For each launch site, add a Circle object based on its coordinate (Lat, Long) values. 
# In addition, add Launch site name as a popup label

for index, row in launch_sites_df.iterrows():
    
    #define the position and name of sites
    lat = row['Lat']
    long = row['Long']
    launch_site = row['Launch Site']
    
    #Define the Circle object
    circle = folium.Circle(
                #properties of circle (location, radius, color, fill)
                [lat, long],
                radius=50, 
                color='#d35400', 
                fill=True
            ).add_child(folium.Popup(launch_site)) #popup label of site name
    
    #Add the Circle objects to the map (in the loop)
    site_map.add_child(circle)
    
site_map

Cell 36

# Function to assign color to launch outcome
def assign_marker_color(launch_outcome):
    # If class=1, marker_color value will be green
    # If class=0, marker_color value will be red
    if launch_outcome == 1:
        return 'green'
    else:
        return 'red'
    
spacex_df['marker_color'] = spacex_df['class'].apply(assign_marker_color)
spacex_df.tail(10)

Launch Site Lat Long class marker_color
46 KSC LC-39A 28.573255 -80.646895 1 green

49 CCAFS SLC-40 28.563197 -80.576820 1 green
50 CCAFS SLC-40 28.563197 -80.576820 1 green

Cell 38

#put launches into clusters and put onto map
marker_cluster = MarkerCluster()
site_map.add_child(marker_cluster) #note, this won't show up unless the individual launches are put onto the map (the next lines of code cover this)


for index, row in spacex_df.iterrows():
    
    #define the position and launch site for each launch
    lat = row['Lat']
    long = row['Long']
    launch_site = row['Launch Site']
    
    #deine the launch marker for each launch
    launch_marker = folium.Marker(
        [lat, long],
        # Create an icon as a text label and colour depending on launch success/failure (marker colour)
        icon=folium.Icon(color='white', icon_color=row['marker_color'])
    )
    
    # Add the launch marker for each launch to the launch clusters (not the map, the launch clusters themselves)
    marker_cluster.add_child(launch_marker)

#show the map zoomed out with the centre at NASA
site_map

Cell 43

# Add Mouse Position to get the coordinate (Lat, Long) for a mouse over on the map
# (This is shown in the top right hand corner)
formatter = "function(num) {return L.Util.formatNum(num, 5);};"
mouse_position = MousePosition(
    position='topright',
    separator=' Long: ',
    empty_string='NaN',
    lng_first=False,
    num_digits=20,
    prefix='Lat:',
    lat_formatter=formatter,
    lng_formatter=formatter,
)

site_map.add_child(mouse_position)
site_map
DataFrame (Cell 43)
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Cell 46

from math import sin, cos, sqrt, atan2, radians

def calculate_distance(lat1, lon1, lat2, lon2):
    # approximate radius of earth in km
    R = 6373.0

    lat1 = radians(lat1)
    lon1 = radians(lon1)
    lat2 = radians(lat2)
    lon2 = radians(lon2)

    dlon = lon2 - lon1
    dlat = lat2 - lat1

    a = sin(dlat / 2)**2 + cos(lat1) * cos(lat2) * sin(dlon / 2)**2
    c = 2 * atan2(sqrt(a), sqrt(1 - a))

    distance = R * c
    return distance

Cell 48

# find coordinate of the closet coastline
launch_site_lat = 28.56319
launch_site_long = -80.57683
launch_site_coordinates = [launch_site_lat, launch_site_long]

coastline_lat = launch_site_lat #same latitude, so the line is directly east
coastline_long = -80.56794
coastline_coordinates = [coastline_lat, coastline_long]

distance_coastline = calculate_distance(launch_site_lat, launch_site_long, coastline_lat, coastline_long)

Cell 51

#define the distance marker (which will be shown at the coastline)
distance_marker = folium.Marker(
    coastline_coordinates,
    icon=DivIcon(
        icon_size=(20,20),
        icon_anchor=(0,0),
        html='<div style="font-size: 12; color:#ff5c33;"><b>%s</b></div>' % "{:10.2f} KM".format(distance_coastline),
        )
    )
#add the distance marker to the map and show the map
site_map.add_child(distance_marker)
site_map
DataFrame (Cell 51)
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Cell 53

#define the distance line (between the coastline and the launch site)
distance_line=folium.PolyLine(
    locations=[launch_site_coordinates, coastline_coordinates],
    weight=1
)

#add the distance line to the map and show the map
site_map.add_child(distance_line)
site_map
DataFrame (Cell 53)
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Cell 64

def distance_and_line(destination_coordinates):
    # Create a marker with distance to a closest city, railway, highway, etc.
    # Draw a line between the marker to the launch site
    
    #calculate the distance between the chosen destination and the launch site defined previously, using the calculate_distance function 
    distance_to_destination = calculate_distance(launch_site_lat, launch_site_long, destination_coordinates[0], destination_coordinates[1])
    
    
    #define the distance marker (which will be shown at the chosen destination)
    distance_marker = folium.Marker(
        destination_coordinates,
        icon=DivIcon(
            icon_size=(20,20),
            icon_anchor=(0,0),
            html='<div style="font-size: 12; color:#ff5c33;"><b>%s</b></div>' % "{:10.2f} KM".format(distance_to_destination),
            )
        )

    #define the distance line (between the chosen destination and the launch site)
    distance_line=folium.PolyLine(
        locations=[launch_site_coordinates, destination_coordinates],
        weight=1
    )

    #add the distance marker to the map 
    site_map.add_child(distance_marker)
    #add the distance line to the map 
    site_map.add_child(distance_line)

    
closest_city_coordinates = [28.10106, -80.6369]
closest_railway_coordinates = [28.57208, -80.58528]
closest_highway_coordinates = [28.56327, -80.57076]

#add distance markers and lines for closest city, railway and highway, using the above function
distance_and_line(closest_city_coordinates)
distance_and_line(closest_railway_coordinates)
distance_and_line(closest_highway_coordinates)

#show the map
site_map
DataFrame (Cell 64)
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Plotly Dash Interactive Dashboard

A Plotly Dash web application was built with two interactive components:

  • Pie Chart: Overall launch success proportion with site filter dropdown
  • Scatter Plot: Payload Mass vs. Landing Outcome by booster version with payload range slider

The dashboard allows filtering by individual launch site or all sites combined, and by payload mass range (0-10,000 kg).

Plotly Dash: Overall Launch Success Rate — Pie chart showing the proportion of successful (green) vs. failed (red) Falcon 9 landings across all launch sites. 66.7% success rate overall.
Plotly Dash: Launch Success Count by Site — Bar chart comparing successful and failed landings at each of the four launch sites: CCAFS SLC 40, KSC LC 39A, VAFB SLC 4E, and CCAFS LC 40.
Plotly Dash: Payload Mass vs. Landing Outcome — Scatter plot showing payload mass (kg) on the x-axis and landing class (0=failure, 1=success) on the y-axis, colored by booster version. Heavier payloads tend to correlate with failures.
Plotly Dash: Success Rate by Orbit Type — Bar chart showing the landing success rate for each orbit type. SSO, ES-L1, and HEO show 100% success; GTO and GEO show lower rates due to heavier payloads.

Key Geospatial Findings

  • All SpaceX launch sites are located within 50 km of the coastline — essential for ocean trajectory and booster landing safety
  • KSC LC 39A (Kennedy Space Center) shows the highest success rate and is the primary site for heavier payloads
  • VAFB SLC 4E in California handles polar orbit missions (SSO), which show high landing success rates
  • CCAFS SLC 40 has the largest total number of launches, accumulating the most mission history
  • All sites are in close proximity to railways and highways — enabling rapid booster recovery and transport logistics
Phase 5

Predictive Analysis: Classification Models

Approach

Four classification algorithms were trained and evaluated using GridSearchCV for hyperparameter tuning with 10-fold cross-validation. The dataset was split 80/20 for training and testing. Features were standardized using StandardScaler. One-hot encoding was applied to categorical features (LaunchSite, Orbit, BoosterVersion).

Cell 40

parameters ={"C":[0.01,0.1,1],'penalty':['l2'], 'solver':['lbfgs']}# l1 lasso l2 ridge
lr=LogisticRegression()

gridsearch_cv_lr = GridSearchCV(lr, parameters, scoring='accuracy', cv=10)
logreg_cv = gridsearch_cv_lr.fit(X_train, Y_train)

sns.set(style="whitegrid")

plt.figure(figsize=(15,8))
sns.barplot(x=algorithms, y=best_scores, palette="Blues")
plt.title("Determining the Best Performing Classification Algorithm")
plt.ylabel("Best Score")
plt.show()
Best Cross-Validation Scores by Algorithm — Comparison of hyperparameter-tuned cross-validation accuracy scores. Decision Tree leads with 90.4% CV accuracy.

Cell 89

plt.figure(figsize=(15,8))
sns.barplot(x=algorithms, y=scores, palette="Blues")
plt.title("Determining the Best Performing Classification Algorithm")
plt.ylabel("Accuracy Score")
plt.show()
Test Set Accuracy Scores by Algorithm — Final test set performance comparison. Decision Tree achieves highest test accuracy at 94.4%, followed by KNN (88.9%).

Model Performance Summary

Algorithm Best CV Accuracy Test Accuracy Best Parameters Status
Logistic Regression 84.6% 83.3% C=0.01, penalty=l2, solver=lbfgs Baseline
Support Vector Machine 84.8% 83.3% C=1.0, gamma=0.032, kernel=sigmoid Comparable
Decision Tree 90.4% 94.4% criterion=entropy, max_depth=6, max_features=sqrt BEST MODEL
K-Nearest Neighbors 87.7% 88.9% criterion=gini, max_depth=14, max_features=auto Strong
Section 10

Key Findings and Conclusion

Data & Feature Findings

Success rate trend: SpaceX's Falcon 9 landing success rate improved dramatically over time, from near 0% before 2015 to consistently above 80% by 2019-2020, reflecting rapid engineering iteration.
Launch site performance: KSC LC 39A shows the highest success rate. CCAFS SLC 40 has the most launches but a lower rate due to early experimental missions.
Orbit type impact: SSO, ES-L1, and HEO orbits show 100% landing success. GTO orbits (geostationary transfer) show lower success rates, likely due to heavier payload requirements.
Payload mass threshold: Heavier payloads (above approximately 6,000 kg) correlate with lower landing success — higher mass leaves less fuel for landing burns.
Booster version improvement: Falcon 9 FT and later block 5 variants show significantly higher success rates than v1.0 and v1.1, indicating design maturity.
Overall success rate: 60 out of 90 launches (66.7%) resulted in successful first stage recovery.

Geospatial Findings

Coastline proximity: All launch sites are within 1 km of the Atlantic or Pacific coastline — enabling ocean trajectories and booster recovery at sea landing zones.
Infrastructure: Every site is within 20 km of major highways and railways, enabling efficient booster transport back to refurbishment facilities.
Equatorial proximity: Florida sites (CCAFS, KSC) are at ~28 degrees latitude, providing Earth's rotational boost for eastward launches and geostationary missions.

Machine Learning Findings

Best model: Decision Tree with entropy criterion and max_depth=6 achieved the highest test accuracy at 94.4%, outperforming Logistic Regression and SVM by over 11 percentage points.
Generalization: Decision Tree's test accuracy (94.4%) exceeds its CV accuracy (90.4%), suggesting the test split was favorable — but the model generalizes well.
SVM vs. Logistic Regression: Both achieved identical test accuracy (83.3%), showing they capture similar linear boundaries in feature space.
KNN performance: KNN achieved 88.9% test accuracy — second best — benefiting from the non-parametric, proximity-based classification approach.
Key predictive features: FlightNumber (experience), PayloadMass, Orbit type (one-hot encoded), and LaunchSite are the most informative features for landing prediction.

Conclusion

This capstone project demonstrates an end-to-end data science pipeline — from raw API data collection through exploratory analysis, interactive visualization, and predictive modeling — applied to the real-world problem of SpaceX Falcon 9 landing prediction.

The Decision Tree classifier with tuned hyperparameters achieves 94.4% accuracy on the test set, making it a highly reliable predictor of first stage landing success. This prediction capability has direct business value: competitors and commercial customers can use such a model to estimate whether a competing SpaceX launch will reduce costs through booster recovery, informing their own pricing strategies.

The analysis confirms that SpaceX's success rate has dramatically improved over time due to technological iteration, and that mission parameters (orbit type, payload mass, launch site, and booster version) are strong predictors of recovery success.
